standards.title: Revenue from Contracts with Customers
keyDifferences: IFRS 15 has a single comprehensive model for revenue recognition identical in core principles to ASC 606.
IFRS does not have the specific industry-specific guidance (e.g. software) that exists in legacy US GAAP, relying more on principle-based application.
Collectibility threshold: IFRS uses 'probable' (which is interpreted as more likely than not), whereas US GAAP uses 'probable' (which is interpreted as around 75-80% likely).
standards.disclosureRequirements: Entities must provide qualitative and quantitative information about contracts with customers, significant judgments, and assets recognized from the costs to obtain or fulfill a contract.

standards.title: Leases
keyDifferences: Under IFRS 16, there is a single lessee accounting model where all leases are treated as finance leases (recognizing right-of-use asset and lease liability).
Under ASC 842, lessees classify leases as either operating or finance, directly impacting the income statement presentation (straight-line vs front-loaded).
IFRS 16 exempts low-value assets and short-term leases from capitalization. US GAAP does not have a low-value asset exemption.

standards.title: Financial Instruments
keyDifferences: Impairment: IFRS 9 uses a 3-stage Expected Credit Loss (ECL) model where 12-month ECL is recognized for Stage 1, while US GAAP CECL recognizes lifetime expected credit losses immediately.
Hedging: IFRS 9 aligns hedge accounting more closely with risk management, whereas US GAAP remains slightly more rules-based.
